Job stands at the edge of a terrible recognition: human life is not only brief but fundamentally fragile. He does not console himself with clichés about mortality. Instead, he names it plainly—we are born of woman, full of trouble, here for a span of days so short that we wither like flowers and fade like shadows. And in this condition of radical weakness, God opens His eyes upon us. Job presses the question into God's face: Why? Why bring such a fleeting, troubled creature into judgment? Why does the Almighty concern Himself with beings He has deliberately limited, whose very days are numbered and held in His hands? There is almost a desperate logic in Job's plea—since we are nothing, let us be nothing in peace. Let us rest like hired hands who have earned their wage and ask nothing more. But Job cannot settle there, because something in him—something stubborn, something that recognizes what he is not supposed to recognize—will not let go of a hope that seems absurd.

The contrast Job draws between the tree and the man cuts to the bone of his despair. A tree that is cut down will sprout again. At the scent of water, it will bud and send forth new shoots. There is renewal written into the very nature of wood and soil and root. But a man? A man lies down and does not rise. When he dies, he is gone as completely as water vanishes from the sea and a river runs dry. Until the heavens themselves are no more, he will not be awakened. Job is not being poetic—he is being honest about what death looks like when you strip away the comfortable distance. The finality is absolute. And yet, in the midst of this darkness, Job asks a question that transforms everything: "When a man dies, will he live again?" The question hangs in the air like a prayer. He cannot answer it with certainty. But he chooses to lean into a hope he cannot see, declaring that he will wait through all his hard service until his renewal comes. He imagines a future in which God will call and he will answer, in which God will desire the work of His hands enough to restore him, in which his sin will be sealed away and his iniquity covered.

The reflection that follows is brutal in its honesty. Job returns to the weight of what he has just dared to hope for, describing how God destroys a man's hope the way mountains crumble and rocks are dislodged—slowly, relentlessly, inevitably. A man passes away, his countenance is changed, he is sent into that final silence. And the cruelty of it is that he knows nothing of what comes after. If his children rise to honor, he does not see it. If they fall into disgrace, he does not mourn it. Death cuts him off not only from life but from every thread that connected him to meaning and purpose beyond himself. He feels only the pain of his own body. This is what terrifies Job most—not just death, but the isolation of death, the way it severs us from everything we have lived for. Yet Job has already spoken his defiance into this void. He has asked whether a man will live again, and he has declared that he will wait for his renewal, that God will call and he will answer. He has no proof. He has only the audacity to believe that the God who numbered his days and set his limits might also be the God who calls the dead to rise.
